Their PS1 bag, introduced in 2008, was one of the defining It Bags of the decade: a clean rectangular structured bag with a buckle closure and a school-bag heritage that sat perfectly between commercial and cool. The house has become one of the most respected in American fashion, producing collections that combine technical precision with a kind of downtown New York unpredictability. They work with fabric manufacturers to develop exclusive textiles and have built a reputation for craftsmanship that places them in a different category from most of their American contemporaries. Proenza Schouler is the standard for what serious American fashion can look like.

name: Chloé
designer: Gaby Aghion / Karl Lagerfeld / Phoebe Philo / Clare Waight Keller
description: Gaby Aghion founded Chloé in Paris in 1952 with the goal of making luxury prêt-à-porter — ready-to-wear with couture sensibility — at a time when serious fashion existed only in the made-to-measure ateliers of the great houses. Karl Lagerfeld joined in 1966 and ran the house for most of the next two decades, producing romantic, fluid collections that defined a particular idea of effortless Parisian femininity. Stella McCartney and then Phoebe Philo led celebrated revivals. Clare Waight Keller's decade produced some of the most critically admired ready-to-wear in modern fashion. Gabriela Hearst now leads a sustainability-focused iteration. Chloé's signature blends — ethereal fabrics, warm colors, a sense of movement — have remained remarkably consistent across seventy years and ten creative directors. The house keeps finding new ways to look like itself.

name: Moschino
description: Franco Moschino founded his house in Milan in 1983 and spent a decade using fashion as a vehicle for satirizing fashion — Couture is not for cows written on a jacket, question marks replacing buttons, quilted chain-link handbags made from packaging tape. He died in 1994 at 44 and left a house with no clear plan for succession. Jeremy Scott became creative director in 2013 and executed the wildest possible interpretation of Moschino's spirit: Barbie runway shows, McDonald's-branded couture, inflatable dresses, cartoon violence translated into silk organza. Scott's Moschino was either the most fun fashion has been in thirty years or the most exhausting, depending on your taste. He left in 2023. The house continues looking for someone to carry the joke forward. Moschino is proof that fashion can take itself completely seriously about not taking itself seriously.

name: Zara
description: Amancio Ortega opened the first Zara in La Coruña, Spain in 1975 and built it into the world's largest fashion retailer by inventing fast fashion as an operational model rather than a cultural aspiration. Zara's supply chain can move a design from sketch to store in two weeks — a speed that allows it to interpret runway trends while they're still happening and deliver them to high streets globally before the season changes. Its creative model is deliberately derivative and its environmental record is complicated, but its understanding of what women want to wear and how quickly has been unmatched for thirty years. Inditex, the parent company, is now one of the wealthiest companies in Europe. Ortega is one of the wealthiest people in the world. Zara democratized fashion. What that democracy cost is the conversation the industry is still having.

name: The Beret
description: The beret is one of the oldest and most widely traveled hats in human history — a Basque peasant's working head covering that became the costume of French intellectuals, Che Guevara's revolutionary uniform, the Parisian artist's trademark, and the defining accessory of a certain kind of studied nonchalance that fashion has exported globally. Laulhère in the French Basque Country has been making berets since 1840 by the same felting and shrinking process — a single flat disk of wool becoming a three-dimensional form — and remains the benchmark for quality. Worn at the back of the head in the Parisian manner, tilted to one side in the beatnik tradition, or pulled down over one ear in a military reference, the beret changes with the wearer in a way that few hats achieve. It is the most portable and least complicated statement in fashion.
